content:
# Numbers Chapter 6 — The EMET Bible (Literal)

**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), pp. 153–154; Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 115–116 (LXX) | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/NUM_006_page_001.jpg`, `NUM_006_page_002.jpg`; `source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/NUM_006_vaticanus_page_001.jpg`, `NUM_006_vaticanus_page_002.jpg`

---

> **MANUSCRIPT OBSERVATION — THE NAZIRITE VOW AND THE AARONIC BLESSING:** The Leningrad Codex continues on pages 153–154 with two major units: the law of the Nazirite (vv. 1–21) and the priestly blessing (vv. 22–27). The parashah marker is visible in the manuscript between these sections. The chapter is governed by the term נָזִיר (nazir, "one separated/consecrated"), from the root נזר (nazar, "to separate/dedicate"). The three marks of the Nazirite are abstention from wine and grape products, avoidance of corpse contamination, and the uncut hair — the נֵזֶר (nezer, "consecration/crown") of God upon his head. The concluding Aaronic blessing (vv. 24–26) is one of the most ancient liturgical texts in the Hebrew Bible, confirmed by two silver scrolls from Ketef Hinnom (late seventh/early sixth century BCE) bearing abbreviated forms of this blessing. The Vaticanus LXX (pp. 115–116) follows the MT closely throughout, rendering נάζιρ as a transliteration and YHWH as κύριος.

---

## The Law of the Nazirite (vv. 1–8)

**1** And-spoke [va-yedabber] YHWH unto [el] Moses [Mosheh], saying [le'mor]:

**2** "Speak [dabber] unto [el] the-Sons of [benei] Israel [Yisra'el], and-you-shall-say [ve-amarta] unto [alehem] them: A-man [ish] or [o] a-woman [ishah], when [ki] he-makes-extraordinary [yafli'] to-vow [lindor] a-vow of [neder] a-Nazirite [nazir], to-separate-himself [le-hazzir] to-YHWH [la-YHWH] —

> **Key terms:** יַפְלִא (yafli') = "he makes extraordinary/does-wondrously" — the hiphil of פלא (pala', "to be wonderful/extraordinary"), suggesting that the Nazirite vow is an exceptional act of devotion beyond normal obligation. נָזִיר (nazir) = "a separated one/consecrated one" — from נזר (nazar, "to separate"). The vow is explicitly open to both men and women (אִישׁ אוֹ אִשָּׁה, ish o ishah), one of the few ritual roles in the Torah accessible to both sexes. **LXX:** ὃς ἐὰν μεγάλως εὔξηται εὐχήν ("whoever greatly vows a vow") — μεγάλως ("greatly") for יַפְלִא, interpreting the extraordinary nature as magnitude. ἀφαγνίσασθαι ἁγνείαν ("to purify a purification") for לְהַזִּיר.

**3** From-wine [mi-yayin] and-strong-drink [ve-shekhar] he-shall-separate-himself [yazzir]; vinegar of [khomets] wine [yayin] and-vinegar of [ve-khomets] strong-drink [shekhar] he-shall-not [lo'] drink [yishteh]; and-any [ve-khol] juice of [mishrat] grapes [anavim] he-shall-not [lo'] drink [yishteh]; and-grapes [anavim], fresh [lakhim] or [vi-] dried [yeveshim], he-shall-not [lo'] eat [yo'khal].

> **Key terms:** שֵׁכָר (shekhar) = "strong drink/intoxicant" — any fermented beverage other than wine, possibly including date-beer or barley-beer. חֹמֶץ (khomets) = "vinegar" — the soured form of wine or strong drink is also prohibited, extending the ban beyond the pleasurable to include even the degraded by-products. מִשְׁרַת עֲנָבִים (mishrat anavim) = "juice/soaking of grapes" — a rare word, from שׁרה (sharah, "to soak/steep"); the prohibition covers even non-fermented grape juice. לַחִים (lakhim) = "fresh/moist" and יְבֵשִׁים (yeveshim) = "dried" — raisins are included. **LXX:** ὅσα κατεργάζεται ἐξ ἀμπέλου ("whatever is produced from the vine") — a summary rendering for the grape products.

**4** All [kol] the-days of [yemei] his-separation [nizro] from-anything [mi-kol] which [asher] is-made [ye'aseh] from-the-vine of [mi-gefen] the-wine [ha-yayin], from-seeds [me-khartsannim] and-unto [ve-ad] skin [zag], he-shall-not [lo'] eat [yo'khal].

> **Key terms:** חַרְצַנִּים (khartsannim) = "seeds/pits" of the grape. זָג (zag) = "skin/husk" — together these two terms establish the absolute scope of the prohibition: literally everything from the grapevine, from the innermost seed to the outermost skin. These terms occur only here in the Hebrew Bible, making their precise meaning dependent on context and tradition. The Talmud (b. Nazir 34b) debates which is the inner part and which the outer. **LXX:** ἀπὸ στεμφύλων ἕως γιγάρτου ("from grape-skins to grape-seed") — στέμφυλα = pressed grape skins; γίγαρτον = grape seed.

**5** All [kol] the-days of [yemei] the-vow of [neder] his-separation [nizro], a-razor [ta'ar] shall-not [lo'] pass [ya'avor] upon [al] his-head [ro'sho]; until [ad] the-fullness of [melo't] the-days [ha-yamim] which [asher] he-separates-himself [yazzir] to-YHWH [la-YHWH], holy [qadosh] he-shall-be [yihyeh]; he-shall-let-grow-freely [gadel] [he shall let grow] the-locks of [pera'] the-hair of [se'ar] his-head [ro'sho].

> **Key terms:** תַּעַר (ta'ar) = "razor" — the same prohibition given to Samson (Judg 13:5; 16:17). פֶּרַע שְׂעַר רֹאשׁוֹ (pera' se'ar ro'sho) = "the locks of the hair of his head" — פֶּרַע (pera') = "loose/unbound hair," the same word used of the uncovered hair of the sotah (5:18) and of the mourning leper (Lev 13:45). Here, however, the wild growth is a sign of consecration, not degradation. The Nazirite's hair is called קָדוֹשׁ (qadosh, "holy") — it is the visible sign of the vow's duration. **LXX:** ξυρὸν οὐκ ἐπελεύσεται ἐπὶ τὴν κεφαλήν ("a razor shall not come upon the head"); ἅγιος ἔσται τρέφων κόμην τρίχα τῆς κεφαλῆς ("holy he shall be, growing the hair of the head").

**6** All [kol] the-days of [yemei] his-separating [hazziro] to-YHWH [la-YHWH], upon [al] a-soul of [nefesh] a-dead-one [met] he-shall-not [lo'] come [yavo'].

> **Key terms:** נֶפֶשׁ מֵת (nefesh met) = "a soul of a dead one" = a dead body. The Nazirite shares with the high priest (Lev 21:11) the prohibition against all corpse contact, even for immediate family. The term נֶפֶשׁ here means "body/person" (a dead person), illustrating the semantic range of this word beyond "soul." **LXX:** ἐπὶ πάσῃ ψυχῇ τετελευτηκυίᾳ ("upon any soul having died") — ψυχή for נֶפֶשׁ.

**7** For-his-father [le-aviv] and-for-his-mother [u-le-immo], for-his-brother [le-akhiv] and-for-his-sister [u-le-akhoto], he-shall-not [lo'] make-himself-unclean [yittamma'] for-them [lahem] in-their-dying [be-motam]; for [ki] the-consecration of [nezer] his-God [Elohav] [is] upon [al] his-head [ro'sho].

> **Key terms:** נֵזֶר אֱלֹהָיו (nezer Elohav) = "the consecration/crown of his God" — נֵזֶר (nezer) is the same word used for the high priest's golden crown plate (Exod 29:6; Lev 8:9) and for the royal crown (2 Sam 1:10; 2 Kgs 11:12). The Nazirite's uncut hair functions as a living crown of dedication. The list of relatives — father, mother, brother, sister — matches exactly the list in the high priest's corpse-prohibition (Lev 21:11), reinforcing the parallel between the Nazirite and the high priest. **LXX:** ὅτι εὐχὴ θεοῦ αὐτοῦ ἐπ' αὐτῷ ("because the vow of his God is upon him") — εὐχή ("vow") for נֵזֶר, an interpretive rendering.

**8** All [kol] the-days of [yemei] his-separation [nizro], holy [qadosh] he [hu'] to-YHWH [la-YHWH].

> **Key terms:** This summary verse states the Nazirite's status in the most concise terms: קָדֹשׁ הוּא לַיהוָה (qadosh hu' la-YHWH) = "holy he [is] to YHWH." The verbless clause (no copula) gives it the force of an absolute declaration. The phrase parallels the inscription on the high priest's turban plate: קֹדֶשׁ לַיהוָה (qodesh la-YHWH, "holy to YHWH," Exod 28:36). **LXX:** ἅγιος ἔσται κυρίῳ ("holy he shall be to [the] Lord").

---

## Defilement and Reconsecration of the Nazirite (vv. 9–12)

**9** And-if [ve-khi] dies [yamut] a-dead-one [met] upon-him [alav] in-suddenness [be-feta'] suddenly [pit'om], and-he-defiles [ve-timme'] [the]-head of [ro'sh] his-consecration [nizro], and-he-shall-shave [ve-gillakh] his-head [ro'sho] on-the-day of [be-yom] his-purification [tohorato]; on-the-day [ba-yom] the-seventh [ha-shevi'i] he-shall-shave-it [yegalekhennu].

> **Key terms:** בְּפֶתַע פִּתְאֹם (be-feta' pit'om) = "in suddenness, suddenly" — a doubled expression of abruptness: an unexpected death occurs in the Nazirite's immediate presence, contaminating him through no deliberate act. רֹאשׁ נִזְרוֹ (ro'sh nizro) = "the head of his consecration" — the hair that bore the nezer is now defiled and must be removed. The seventh-day shaving corresponds to the seven-day purification period for corpse contamination (Num 19:12). **LXX:** ἐξάπινα ... ἐπ' αὐτῷ ("suddenly ... upon him"); ξυρήσεται τὴν κεφαλὴν αὐτοῦ ("he shall shave his head").

**10** And-on-the-day [u-va-yom] the-eighth [ha-shemini] he-shall-bring [yavi'] two [shtei] turtledoves [torim] or [o] two [shnei] sons of [benei] a-dove [yonah] unto [el] the-priest [ha-kohen], unto [el] the-entrance of [petakh] the-Tent of [Ohel] Meeting [Mo'ed].

> **Key terms:** תֹּרִים (torim) = "turtledoves" and בְּנֵי יוֹנָה (benei yonah) = "sons of a dove" = young pigeons — the standard pair offering of the poor (Lev 5:7; 12:8; 14:22). The eighth-day offering parallels the purification sequence for the leper (Lev 14:10) and the post-partum mother (Lev 12:6). **LXX:** δύο τρυγόνας ἢ δύο νεοσσοὺς περιστερῶν ("two turtledoves or two young pigeons").

**11** And-shall-make [ve-asah] the-priest [ha-kohen] one [ekhad] for-a-sin-offering [le-khattat] and-one [ve-ekhad] for-a-burnt-offering [le-olah], and-he-shall-make-atonement [ve-khipper] upon-him [alav] from-that-which [me-asher] he-sinned [khata'] upon [al] the-soul [ha-nafesh]; and-he-shall-sanctify [ve-qiddesh] [et] his-head [ro'sho] on-that [ba-] day [yom] the-that [ha-hu'].

> **Key terms:** מֵאֲשֶׁר חָטָא עַל הַנָּפֶשׁ (me-asher khata' al ha-nafesh) = "from that which he sinned upon/concerning the soul/dead body" — a difficult phrase. The Nazirite "sinned" not by intention but by the fact that his consecrated state was violated by contact with death. The sin-offering addresses the ritual breach, not moral guilt. This verse was cited by Rabbi Eleazar ha-Qappar (b. Ta'anit 11a; b. Nazir 19a) to argue that any Nazirite "sins" by denying himself wine — though this reading is contested. **LXX:** ἀφ' ὧν ἥμαρτεν περὶ τῆς ψυχῆς ("from what he sinned concerning the soul/body").

**12** And-he-shall-separate [ve-hizzir] to-YHWH [la-YHWH] [et] the-days of [yemei] his-separation [nizro], and-he-shall-bring [ve-hevi'] a-lamb [keves] a-son of [ben] its-year [shenato] for-a-guilt-offering [le-asham]; and-the-days [ve-ha-yamim] the-first [ha-rishonim] shall-fall [yippelu], for [ki] his-separation [nizro] was-defiled [tame'].

> **Key terms:** כֶּבֶשׂ בֶּן שְׁנָתוֹ (keves ben shenato) = "a lamb, a son of its year" = a yearling lamb. לְאָשָׁם (le-asham) = "for a guilt-offering" — the asham addresses the sacrilege of the broken vow. וְהַיָּמִים הָרִאשֹׁנִים יִפְּלוּ (ve-ha-yamim ha-rishonim yippelu) = "and the first/former days shall fall" — the previous days of the vow are annulled; the count restarts from zero. The verb נָפַל (nafal, "to fall") here means "to be void/fall away." **LXX:** αἱ ἡμέραι αἱ πρότεραι ἄλογοι ἔσονται ("the former days shall be without account") — ἄλογοι ("uncounted") is an interpretive rendering of יִפְּלוּ.

---

## Completion of the Nazirite Vow (vv. 13–21)

**13** And-this [ve-zo't] [is] the-law of [torat] the-Nazirite [ha-nazir]: on-the-day of [be-yom] the-fullness of [melo't] the-days of [yemei] his-separation [nizro], he-shall-bring [yavi'] [et]-him [oto] unto [el] the-entrance of [petakh] the-Tent of [Ohel] Meeting [Mo'ed].

> **Key terms:** תּוֹרַת הַנָּזִיר (torat ha-nazir) = "the law/instruction of the Nazirite" — the procedural heading for the completion ritual, parallel to תּוֹרַת הַקְּנָאֹת (5:29). יָבִיא אֹתוֹ (yavi' oto) = "he shall bring him" — the object suffix is ambiguous: "he shall bring himself" (reflexive) or "one shall bring him" (impersonal passive); the context suggests the Nazirite presents himself. **LXX:** προσοίσει αὐτὸν ("he shall bring him") — maintains the ambiguity.

**14** And-he-shall-bring-near [ve-hiqriv] [et] his-offering [qorbano] to-YHWH [la-YHWH]: one [ekhad] lamb [keves] a-son of [ben] its-year [shenato], unblemished [tamim], for-a-burnt-offering [le-olah]; and-one [ve-khavsah] ewe-lamb [akhat], a-daughter of [bat] its-year [shenatah], unblemished [temimah], for-a-sin-offering [le-khattat]; and-one [ve-] ram [ayil ekhad], unblemished [tamim], for-peace-offerings [li-shelamim];

> **Key terms:** The Nazirite's completion offering is a full three-part sacrifice: עֹלָה (olah, "burnt offering") — total consecration; חַטָּאת (khattat, "sin offering") — purification; שְׁלָמִים (shelamim, "peace offerings") — communion/fellowship. The complement of male lamb + female lamb + ram mirrors the full scope of the sacrificial system. כַּבְשָׂה (khavsah) = "ewe-lamb," the feminine of כֶּבֶשׂ. **LXX:** ἄμνον ἐνιαύσιον ἄμωμον ... ἀμνάδα ἐνιαυσίαν ἄμωμον ... κριὸν ἕνα ἄμωμον ("a yearling lamb unblemished ... a yearling ewe-lamb unblemished ... one ram unblemished").

**15** And-a-basket of [ve-sal] unleavened-breads [matstsot], cakes of [khallot] fine-flour [solet] mixed [belulot] with-oil [ba-shemen], and-wafers of [u-reqiqei] unleavened-bread [matstsot] anointed [meshukhim] with-oil [ba-shamen]; and-their-grain-offering [u-minkhatam] and-their-drink-offerings [ve-niskeihem].

> **Key terms:** סַל מַצּוֹת (sal matstsot) = "a basket of unleavened breads" — the same term used for the priestly ordination basket (Exod 29:3; Lev 8:2). חַלֹּת (khallot) = "cakes/loaves" (perforated breads). רְקִיקֵי (reqiqei) = "wafers" (thin flat breads). The two types — mixed with oil and anointed with oil — parallel the grain offering regulations of Leviticus 2. **LXX:** κανοῦν ἀζύμων ("a basket of unleavened breads"); σεμιδάλεως ("of fine flour") for סֹלֶת.

**16** And-shall-bring-near [ve-hiqriv] the-priest [ha-kohen] before [lifnei] YHWH, and-he-shall-make [ve-asah] [et] his-sin-offering [khattato] and [ve-et] his-burnt-offering [olato].

**17** And [ve-et] the-ram [ha-ayil] he-shall-make [ya'aseh] a-sacrifice of [zevakh] peace-offerings [shelamim] to-YHWH [la-YHWH], upon [al] the-basket of [sal] the-unleavened-breads [ha-matstsot]; and-shall-make [ve-asah] the-priest [ha-kohen] [et] its-grain-offering [minkhato] and [ve-et] its-drink-offering [nisko].

> **Key terms:** The order is significant: sin offering first (purification), then burnt offering (total dedication), then peace offering (communion). The peace-offering ram is offered "upon" (עַל, al) the basket of unleavened bread — the bread accompanies the sacrifice as prescribed in Lev 7:12–13. **LXX:** θυσίαν σωτηρίου ("sacrifice of salvation/deliverance") for זֶבַח שְׁלָמִים.

**18** And-shall-shave [ve-gillakh] the-Nazirite [ha-nazir] [at] the-entrance of [petakh] the-Tent of [Ohel] Meeting [Mo'ed] [et] the-head of [ro'sh] his-consecration [nizro]; and-he-shall-take [ve-laqakh] [et] the-hair of [se'ar] the-head of [ro'sh] his-consecration [nizro], and-he-shall-put [ve-natan] [it] upon [al] the-fire [ha-esh] which [asher] [is] under [takhat] the-sacrifice of [zevakh] the-peace-offerings [ha-shelamim].

> **Key terms:** The consecrated hair is burned under the peace offering — not destroyed as waste but consumed in the sacrificial fire, returning the nezer to YHWH. The shaving takes place at the entrance of the Tent of Meeting, making it a public, sacral act. The fire "under the peace offering" is the altar fire; the hair ascends with the sacrifice. This is the ritual release of the vow: what was set apart is now returned to its source. **LXX:** ξυρήσεται ... τὴν κεφαλὴν τῆς εὐχῆς αὐτοῦ ... ἐπὶ τὸ πῦρ τὸ ὑπὸ τὴν θυσίαν τοῦ σωτηρίου ("he shall shave ... the head of his vow ... upon the fire under the sacrifice of salvation").

**19** And-shall-take [ve-laqakh] the-priest [ha-kohen] [et] the-shoulder [ha-zero'a], the-boiled [beshelah], from [min] the-ram [ha-ayil], and-one [ve-khallat] cake of [khallat] unleavened-bread [matstsah] one [akhat] from [min] the-basket [ha-sal], and-one [u-] wafer of [reqiq] unleavened-bread [matstsah] one [ekhad]; and-he-shall-put [ve-natan] [them] upon [al] the-palms of [kappei] the-Nazirite [ha-nazir] after [akhar] his-shaving [hitgallekho] [et] his-consecration [nizro].

> **Key terms:** הַזְּרֹעַ בְּשֵׁלָה (ha-zero'a beshelah) = "the shoulder, the boiled [one]" — the front leg of the ram, boiled (not roasted). In normal peace offerings the priest receives the breast and right thigh (Lev 7:32–34); here the shoulder is an additional priestly portion unique to the Nazirite ritual. הִתְגַּלְּחוֹ (hitgallekho) = "his shaving himself" — the hitpa'el infinitive, indicating the Nazirite's own agency in the shaving. **LXX:** τὸν βραχίονα ἑφθὸν ("the boiled arm/shoulder") for הַזְּרֹעַ בְּשֵׁלָה.

**20** And-shall-wave [ve-henif] them [otam] the-priest [ha-kohen] [as] a-wave-offering [tenufah] before [lifnei] YHWH; holy [qodesh] it [hu'] for-the-priest [la-kohen], upon [al] the-breast of [khazeh] the-wave-offering [ha-tenufah] and-upon [ve-al] the-thigh of [shoq] the-contribution [ha-terumah]; and-after [ve-akhar] [that] the-Nazirite [ha-nazir] may-drink [yishteh] wine [yayin].

> **Key terms:** The waving before YHWH is the tenufah gesture — the ritual presentation. The items become קֹדֶשׁ (qodesh, "holy") for the priest, in addition to the standard priestly portions (the breast and thigh). The final clause — וְאַחַר יִשְׁתֶּה הַנָּזִיר יָיִן (ve-akhar yishteh ha-nazir yayin, "and after [that] the Nazirite may drink wine") — formally releases the Nazirite from the vow. The prohibition that opened the chapter (v. 3) is now lifted. **LXX:** μετὰ ταῦτα πίεται ὁ ηὐγμένος οἶνον ("after these things the one who has vowed may drink wine").

**21** This [zo't] [is] the-law of [torat] the-Nazirite [ha-nazir] who [asher] vows [yiddor] his-offering [qorbano] to-YHWH [la-YHWH] upon [al] his-separation [nizro], apart-from [mi-levad] [that] which [asher] his-hand [yado] attains [tassig]; according-to [ke-fi] his-vow [nidro] which [asher] he-vows [yiddor], so [ken] he-shall-do [ya'aseh] upon [al] the-law of [torat] his-separation [nizro].

> **Key terms:** מִלְּבַד אֲשֶׁר תַּשִּׂיג יָדוֹ (mi-levad asher tassig yado) = "apart from [that] which his hand attains" — the prescribed offerings are the minimum; a Nazirite may bring additional offerings according to his means. The phrase תַּשִּׂיג יָדוֹ ("his hand reaches/attains") is the standard idiom for financial capacity (Lev 14:21–22). This closing formula (תּוֹרַת הַנָּזִיר, torat ha-nazir) brackets the entire section with v. 13. **LXX:** πλὴν ὅσα ἂν εὕρῃ ἡ χεὶρ αὐτοῦ ("except whatever his hand may find").

---

## The Aaronic Blessing (vv. 22–27)

**22** And-spoke [va-yedabber] YHWH unto [el] Moses [Mosheh], saying [le'mor]:

**23** "Speak [dabber] unto [el] Aaron [Aharon] and-unto [ve-el] his-sons [banav], saying [le'mor]: Thus [koh] you-shall-bless [tevaraku] [et] the-Sons of [benei] Israel [Yisra'el]; say [amor] to-them [lahem]:

> **Key terms:** כֹּה תְבָרֲכוּ (koh tevaraku) = "thus you shall bless" — the koh ("thus/in this manner") fixes the wording: the blessing must be spoken in exactly these words. אָמוֹר לָהֶם (amor lahem) = "say to them" — the infinitive absolute used as an imperative, giving the force of a perpetual command. **LXX:** οὕτως εὐλογήσετε ("thus you shall bless"); λέγοντες αὐτοῖς ("saying to them").

**24** May-bless-you [yevarekh-kha] YHWH and-may-he-keep-you [ve-yishmerekha].

> **Key terms:** יְבָרֶכְךָ (yevarekh-kha) = "may he bless you" — the jussive (wish/prayer form) of ברך. וְיִשְׁמְרֶךָ (ve-yishmerekha) = "and may he keep/guard you" — שׁמר (shamar) encompasses protection, watchfulness, and covenant faithfulness. The first line of the blessing has three Hebrew words and fifteen letters. **LXX:** εὐλογήσαι σε κύριος καὶ φυλάξαι σε ("may [the] Lord bless you and guard you").

**25** May-cause-to-shine [ya'er] YHWH his-face [panav] unto-you [eleikha] and-may-he-be-gracious-to-you [vi-ykhunnekka].

> **Key terms:** יָאֵר (ya'er) = "may he cause to shine/illuminate" — the hiphil jussive of אור ('or, "to be light"). The shining face is the opposite of the hidden face (Ps 13:2; 27:9): divine favor, presence, and delight. פָּנָיו (panav) = "his face/faces" — the plural construct of פָּנִים (panim); the face of God is the concentrated expression of his person turned toward the worshiper. וִיחֻנֶּךָּ (vi-ykhunnekka) = "and may he be gracious to you" — from חנן (khanan, "to show favor/grace"), the root of חֵן (khen, "grace"). The second line has five Hebrew words and twenty letters. **LXX:** ἐπιφάναι κύριος τὸ πρόσωπον αὐτοῦ ἐπὶ σὲ καὶ ἐλεήσαι σε ("may [the] Lord cause his face to shine upon you and have mercy on you").

**26** May-lift [yissa'] YHWH his-face [panav] unto-you [eleikha] and-may-he-set [ve-yasem] for-you [lekha] peace [shalom].

> **Key terms:** יִשָּׂא (yissa') = "may he lift up" — from נשׂא (nasa', "to lift/carry"). To "lift the face toward" someone is to look upon them with favor and attention (contrast "to fall the face" = displeasure, Gen 4:5–6). שָׁלוֹם (shalom) = "peace/wholeness/completeness" — the culminating word of the blessing. Shalom encompasses not merely the absence of conflict but total well-being: health, prosperity, harmony, and the fullness of covenant relationship. The third line has seven Hebrew words and twenty-five letters. The three lines together form an ascending pattern: 3-5-7 words, 15-20-25 letters — a deliberate literary structure. **LXX:** ἐπάραι κύριος τὸ πρόσωπον αὐτοῦ ἐπὶ σὲ καὶ δῴη σοι εἰρήνην ("may [the] Lord lift his face upon you and give you peace").

**27** And-they-shall-put [ve-samu] [et] my-name [shemi] upon [al] the-Sons of [benei] Israel [Yisra'el], and-I [va-ani] shall-bless-them [avarakhem]."

> **Key terms:** וְשָׂמוּ אֶת שְׁמִי (ve-samu et shemi) = "and they shall put/place my name" — the priests do not bless by their own authority; they place YHWH's name upon the people, and YHWH himself does the blessing. The final clause — וַאֲנִי אֲבָרֲכֵם (va-ani avarakhem) = "and I, I shall bless them" — the emphatic pronoun אֲנִי (ani, "I") makes explicit that the ultimate source of blessing is YHWH, not the priestly intermediary. The structure is: the priests speak the words, YHWH enacts the reality. **LXX:** καὶ ἐπιθήσουσιν τὸ ὄνομά μου ἐπὶ τοὺς υἱοὺς Ισραηλ, καὶ ἐγὼ κύριος εὐλογήσω αὐτούς ("and they shall place my name upon the sons of Israel, and I [the] Lord shall bless them") — the LXX adds κύριος ("Lord"), making YHWH's identity as blesser even more explicit.

---

## EMET's Textual Notes

1. **The Nazirite as a lay-priest (vv. 1–8):** The three prohibitions of the Nazirite — no grape products, no corpse contact, no cutting of hair — correspond directly to priestly restrictions, particularly those of the high priest. The high priest wears the נֵזֶר (nezer, "crown/diadem") of gold (Exod 29:6); the Nazirite wears the nezer of uncut hair. The high priest may not approach any dead body, even a parent (Lev 21:11); the Nazirite shares this exact restriction (v. 7). The term קָדוֹשׁ (qadosh, "holy") applied to the Nazirite (v. 8) is the standard designation for priestly sanctity. The Nazirite vow thus creates a temporary, voluntary form of priestly consecration open to any Israelite, male or female — a remarkable democratization of holiness within the priestly system. The Leningrad Codex text on page 153 is clearly legible for all three prohibition sections.

2. **The word-count and letter-count structure of the Aaronic Blessing (vv. 24–26):** The three lines of the blessing exhibit a precise ascending pattern: 3 words / 15 letters; 5 words / 20 letters; 7 words / 25 letters. Each line adds two words and five letters to the previous one. This mathematical structure was noted by the Masoretes and is visible in the careful spacing of the Leningrad Codex, where the three lines are set apart from the surrounding text. The ascending pattern reflects the theological movement: from blessing and protection (basic provision), through illumination and grace (relational intimacy), to the lifting of YHWH's face and shalom (complete fullness). The Ketef Hinnom silver scrolls (KH1 and KH2), discovered in Jerusalem in 1979 and dated to the late seventh or early sixth century BCE, contain abbreviated forms of this blessing — making them the oldest known texts of the Hebrew Bible.

3. **נֵזֶר (nezer) — the shared vocabulary of crown and consecration (vv. 7, 9, 12, 13, 18, 19, 21):** The word נֵזֶר appears seven times in this chapter, functioning as a key term that weaves the passage together. Its semantic field includes: (a) "separation/consecration" — the state of the Nazirite's vow; (b) "crown/diadem" — the royal and priestly headpiece; (c) the uncut hair itself as a visible sign. The word derives from the root נזר (nazar), which carries the double meaning of "to separate" and "to consecrate." In the broader Hebrew Bible, nezer designates the high priest's golden plate (Exod 29:6), the king's crown (2 Sam 1:10; 2 Kgs 11:12; Ps 89:40 [39]), and — here — the Nazirite's hair-crown. The Vaticanus LXX renders it variously as εὐχή ("vow"), ἁγνεία ("purity"), and ἅγιος ("holy"), never finding a single Greek equivalent for the Hebrew term's layered meaning.

4. **The defiled Nazirite and the guilt offering (vv. 9–12):** The contaminated Nazirite must bring a guilt offering (אָשָׁם, asham) — the same sacrifice required for sacrilege against holy things (Lev 5:14–16). This categorizes the broken Nazirite vow as מְעִילָה (me'ilah), trespass against the sacred, even though the contamination was accidental (בְּפֶתַע פִּתְאֹם, "in sudden suddenness"). The previous days of the vow "fall away" (יִפְּלוּ, yippelu) — they are voided — and the count must restart entirely. The severity of this consequence underscores the weight of the vow: partial completion has no standing; only a complete, unbroken period of consecration fulfills the obligation. The LXX Vaticanus reads αἱ ἡμέραι αἱ πρότεραι ἄλογοι ἔσονται ("the former days shall be without account"), interpreting יִפְּלוּ as rendering the days null.

5. **The boiled shoulder — הַזְּרֹעַ בְּשֵׁלָה (ha-zero'a beshelah) (v. 19):** This is the only instance in the Torah where a specific limb of a peace-offering animal is singled out as "boiled" (בְּשֵׁלָה, beshelah). Normal priestly portions from peace offerings are the breast (חָזֶה, khazeh) and the right thigh (שׁוֹק הַיָּמִין, shoq ha-yamin) per Leviticus 7:32–34. The Nazirite offering adds the shoulder (זְרֹעַ, zero'a) as an extra portion. The Mishnah (Nazir 6:9) discusses the priestly handling of this unique element. The term זְרֹעַ (zero'a, "arm/foreleg/shoulder") is also used metaphorically for divine power (Deut 4:34; Isa 53:1), though the connection here is purely cultic.

6. **וַאֲנִי אֲבָרֲכֵם — "And I, I shall bless them" (v. 27):** The emphatic first-person pronoun אֲנִי (ani) preceding the verb creates a strong contrast with the priests' role: the priests pronounce the words, but YHWH performs the blessing. The verse functions as a divine ratification formula: the priestly blessing is not a human wish but a divinely empowered speech-act. The verb אֲבָרֲכֵם (avarakhem) is in the piel — the intensive/factitive stem — indicating that YHWH's blessing is an effective act that creates the reality it names. The LXX Vaticanus adds κύριος ("Lord") to the clause: καὶ ἐγὼ κύριος εὐλογήσω αὐτούς ("and I, [the] Lord, shall bless them"), an expansion not present in the MT that makes the divine identity explicit.

7. **The juxtaposition of Nazirite law and Aaronic blessing:** The placement of the priestly blessing immediately after the Nazirite legislation is not accidental. The Nazirite section (vv. 1–21) demonstrates that holiness can be voluntarily assumed by any Israelite; the Aaronic blessing (vv. 22–27) demonstrates that divine favor flows through the priestly office to all Israel. Together they present two channels of holiness: ascending (the individual's self-consecration to YHWH through the Nazirite vow) and descending (YHWH's blessing flowing through the priests to the people). The Nazirite reaches up; the blessing comes down. The chapter thus forms a chiastic theology of holiness: human dedication and divine grace meeting at the Tent of Meeting.

8. **LXX Vaticanus distinctive readings (pp. 115–116):** The Vaticanus text shows the following noteworthy features in Numbers 6: (a) εὐχή ("vow") and ἁγνεία ("purity") are used interchangeably for נֵזֶר, showing the translator's difficulty with the term's dual sense; (b) μεγάλως εὔξηται ("greatly vows") for יַפְלִא ("makes extraordinary") in v. 2 — interpreting the extraordinary nature as intensity of commitment; (c) the addition of κύριος in v. 27 as noted above; (d) the three lines of the Aaronic blessing (vv. 24–26) are rendered with close structural fidelity, preserving the ascending three-line form. Overall, the LXX of Numbers 6 shows a translator working carefully to maintain both the legal precision of the Nazirite regulations and the liturgical cadence of the blessing.
